text:
Hello everyone,
I'm writing to make an important product stance explicit: mongosync must not be used for disaster recovery, continuous sync, analytics replicas, or maintaining standby clusters. This applies to both Atlas and
Enterprise Advanced (EA) deployments.
We have already seen customer impact from this misunderstanding. If you have a customer using or planning to use mongosync for DR, engage them immediately to course-correct.
Some earlier MongoDB materials and customer conversations may have created the impression that mongosync could support DR-style patterns. That guidance was wrong. mongosync is a one-time migration tool, and the
current documentation is explicit that Disaster Recovery, Analytics, and similar continuous synchronization workflows are not supported.
Why It Doesn't Work
Two architectural constraints make it fundamentally unsafe for DR:
1. The destination is not guaranteed to be consistent while sync is running.
mongosync reorders operations for performance. As a result, the destination cluster is not guaranteed to match the source, including a stale version of the source, at any point while sync is running, even if sync is
paused.
2. The destination cannot be used for DR failover if the source is lost.
mongosync can support a planned cutover when the source cluster is still available and the migration can be committed. That is not the DR scenario people are trying to solve. In a disaster, the source may be
unavailable, and mongosync cannot safely promote the destination as a standby replacement for the lost source. This applies to both Atlas and EA deployments.
The mongosync FAQ states this explicitly: "Disaster Recovery, Analytics, and other similar use cases are not supported workflows for mongosync."
What To Use Instead
Standby Clusters is the product direction for active/passive DR across Atlas and EA. It is entering Private Preview for both deployment models, with exact timing and eligibility subject to change. Current design targets are
RTO under one minute and RPO under one second, with planned failover designed for zero data loss.
Do not make customer commitments on Standby Clusters availability, scope, or production readiness without engaging the Standby Clusters team first. To nominate customers for Private Preview, contact standby:
preview@mongodb.com.
For customers who need DR today, use supported DR options for their deployment type:
• Backup and restore
• For Atlas: properly designed multi-region clusters with 3 regions, where the architecture satisfies the customer's RTO/RPO requirements, plus the guidance in the Atlas DR Architecture Center
• For EA: validated multi-site architecture and operational runbooks that meet the customer's RTO/RPO requirements. Engage the account team, SA, PS, and Product Management before giving customer-specific
guidance.
For more details, see:
• Atlas Standby Clusters 1-Pager
• EA Standby Clusters 1-Pager
• Atlas Standby Clusters Internal FAQ
• EA Standby Clusters Product Description
• Atlas DR Architecture Center
If a Customer Is Already Using mongosync for DR
1. Engage them now. They may be operating under a false sense of security.
3. Presensupported ate or thee. Depending o post customer legure the is, dius are uly sores mud. gion base, or ustination for Standby Clusters Private Preview.
4. Escalate if needed. If you hit resistance, customer-impact concerns, or contractual issues, loop in account team leadership and Product Management.
Action Items
• Professional Services: Update migration and DR playbooks to include an explicit "no mongosync for DR" guardrail. Flag any active engagement where mongosync is being positioned for DR.
• Solution Consulting: Stop positioning mongosync as a DR solution in any customer conversation, demo, or architecture review. Review any open DR opportunities where mongosync has been discussed and
course-correct with the account team.
• Everyone: If you know of a customer currently running this pattern, raise it with the account team this week. If you are unsure whether a customer's pattern is supported, escalate to Product Management before
giving guidance.
Questions? Reach out to me or Renato Riccio.
